title: NOC 51101 – Conservators and Curators | GTR Immigration
description: Conservators and Curators under NOC 51101 are professional specialists responsible for preserving, restoring, researching, and managing museum and gallery collections across Canada. Classified under TEER 1, this occupation usually requires a university degree and specialized training. These professionals are commonly employed by: Conservators may also be self-employed.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category5 – Occupations in art, culture, recreation and sportTEER1 – Occupations usually require a university degreeMajor Group51 – Professional occupations in art and cultureSub-Major Group511 – Professional occupations in art and cultureMinor Group5110 – Librarians, archivists, conservators and curatorsNOC Code51101

duties: Conservators perform some or all of the following duties:
Examine artifacts and assess their condition
Restore and conserve paintings, photographs, books, sculptures, furniture, pottery, and other artifacts
Advise on display and storage methods
Research and develop new conservation techniques
Provide consultation to museums, galleries, and private collectors
Curators perform some or all of the following duties:
Recommend acquisition of artworks and historical artifacts
Conduct research into construction methods, materials, and structure
Develop exhibition themes and organize displays
Oversee conservation and circulation of collections
Supervise curatorial assistants and museum technicians
employment_requirements: Master’s degree in art conservation or
Completion of a two-year college program in conservation technology
Accreditation by the Canadian Association of Professional Conservators may be required
Bachelor’s or master’s degree in museology, art history, or a related field
Specialization related to collection focus may be required
salary_prospects: $27,900 – $74,200 per year,depending on specialization, province, and institutional size.
With 5,000 projected job openings and slightly fewer job seekers (4,800), NOC 51101 reflects balanced employment prospects across Canada.
Opportunities are strongest in provinces with established cultural sectors such as Ontario, British Columbia, and Quebec.
NOC 51101 is classified under TEER 1, making it generally eligible under:
Eligibility depends on education credential assessment (ECA), language proficiency, and relevant work experience.

The outlook reflects stable demand, particularly in provinces with strong cultural institutions.
Professional careers in Canada’s cultural and heritage sector
